summary: The iPhone 17e is a textbook 'speed bump' update that addresses the 16e's primary shortcoming: the absence of MagSafe. Gruber argues that adding MagSafe alone would have been sufficient for a successful update, but Apple also bumped the SoC from A18 to A19, doubled base storage to 256 GB, and added a new color. The camera hardware remains unchanged year-over-year, though the A19 enables better portrait processing. Compared to the $800 iPhone 17, the 17e sacrifices ProMotion, Camera Control, Dynamic Island, and precision Ultra Wideband — tradeoffs Gruber finds acceptable for price-conscious buyers. He concludes the 17e is now recommendable without hesitation, and speculates Apple may be moving toward annual updates across its entire lineup.
key_points: MagSafe was the 16e's glaring omission, and its addition to the 17e resolves the single biggest reason not to recommend the budget iPhone.
The A19 chip upgrade improves software-driven camera capabilities (portraits, image pipeline) but the camera hardware itself is identical to the 16e — still the weakest in Apple's lineup.
The 17e is limited to first-generation Photographic Styles with baked-in processing, a meaningful downgrade vs. the iPhone 17's non-destructive second-generation styles.
At $600 with 256 GB base storage, the 17e undercuts the iPhone 17's $800 price while offering the same A19 chip, making the old iPhone 16 lineup look increasingly redundant.
Gruber suspects Apple is transitioning to annual speed bumps across its whole lineup, phasing out the practice of selling older models at discounted prices.
money_quotes: Just because the target audience wouldn't miss it — because their old phone didn't have it — doesn't mean they wouldn't miss out by not having it on their new one.
Apple could have stopped there — with the addition of MagSafe alone — and the 17e would've been a successful year-over-year update.
The $599 iPhone 17e, with the A19, benchmarks faster in single-core CPU performance than the $599 MacBook Neo, with the year-old A18 Pro.
The 17e camera is by far the weakest iPhone camera Apple currently offers. For the people considering the 17e, it's probably the best camera of any kind they've ever owned.
I don't think there's anything on par with MagSafe for next year's iPhone 18e.
Frankly, I'm not sure who the year-old iPhone 16 is for today.
With 256 GB of storage, even the base model 17e is recommendable without hesitation.
key_insight: The iPhone 17e earns a clear recommendation by fixing MagSafe — the one meaningful deficiency of its predecessor — while doubling base storage and upgrading the chip, all at the same $600 price.
